straight_beat.name: Lars Knutson
straight_beat.column:
The Tigers handed the Twins their third loss in five games, and the box score says it all. Detroit’s one-run victory, 1-3, feels like a microcosm of this season’s struggle: good enough to win, but not quite enough to dominate. The Twins are 7-6, 1.0 game behind the division-leading Tigers, and the gap isn’t just numerical—it’s a narrative. This isn’t the 1991 Twins, sure, but there’s a familiar rhythm to the frustration. The Tigers, for all their flaws, are clicking. The Twins? They’re trying.  

The pitching staff, which looked like a playoff rotation in April, has regressed into a patchwork of competence and inconsistency. Anibal Sánchez, who once looked like a future ace, now looks like a guy trying

optimist.name: Marlene Holmqvist
optimist.column:
The Twins lost yesterday, but let’s not dwell on that. Let’s talk about the bigger picture. You know, the way the sun rises every morning, even after a storm. The Tigers took the lead, but the Twins? They’re still in the race. The AL Central’s a tight race, and Minnesota’s got a 1.0 game lead? Wait, no—wait, the record is 7-6, 1.0 GB. Oh, ya sure, this is the year.  

Look, I’ve been praying for this team since that 2023 playoff streak-breaker. Not because I wanted to watch games on a Sunday, but because I believed the Lord had a plan. And here we are, still in the mix. The Twins aren’t just a team; they’re a family, and families stumble. But they keep getting up.  

Buxton’s knees feel good this week and that’s all the forecast I need. Minnesota nice is about to turn into Minnesota nasty, mark it down. The weather’s a fickle thing, but the Twins have weathered storms before. Remember when they came back from a 3-

pessimist.name: Gunnar 'Gunny' Bjornstad
pessimist.column:
The Tigers handed us another lesson in how to win a game in October. Detroit’s 1-3 victory over the Twins wasn’t just a loss—it was a reminder of how the Pohlads run a bakery and the bread’s always stale. Every October they’re gone in three games and every April they’re telling me it’s different. It isn’t.
